messages.content:
You are a clinical psychologist writing a psychological case vignette
grounded in Ehlers & Clark's (2000) cognitive model of PTSD.

You have been given a patient's full PTSD profile including their demographics, specific self-reported symptoms, and causal connections between components.
Use ALL of this information to construct a realistic and personalised clinical portrait.

Patient demographics:
  age: 59
  gender: Male
  nationality: North american
  relationship_status: Married
  trauma_type: Bereavement
  pcl5: 34

Patient-reported symptoms per component:
  Triggers: Somatic Pain (Neck): Neck pain, Loss Themes: Talk about people loosing loved ones, Loss of Autonomy: Being forced to do something
  Maladaptive Strategies: Rumination: Revenge: ruminate about how to get even with assailant, Smoking: I smoke, Busyness: I work hard at keeping busy with other things
  Threat: Physical Reactivity: Having strong physical reactions when something reminded you of the stressful experience, Irritability: Irritable behavior, angry outbursts, or acting aggressively, Concentration: Having difficulty concentrating
  Memory: Olfactory Recall: When I think about the frightening event I can sometimes smell things that I smelt when the frightening event happened., Auditory Recall: When I have memories of what happened I sometimes hear things in my head that I heard during the frightening event., Vivid Memory: My memories of the frightening event are very clear and detailed.
  Negative Appraisals: Hypervigilance: I have to be on guard all the time, Constant Vigilance: I have to be especially careful because you never know what can happen next, Permanent Damage: I have permanently changed for the worse

Active causal connections (must appear in the vignette):
  - Triggers → Maladaptive Strategies
  - Triggers → Threat
  - Triggers → Memory
  - Negative Appraisals → Threat
  - Memory → Maladaptive Strategies
  - Memory → Threat
  - Memory → Triggers
  - Threat → Maladaptive Strategies

Forbidden causal connections (must NEVER appear — not explicitly, not implicitly, not narratively):
  - Triggers → Negative Appraisals
  - Negative Appraisals → Maladaptive Strategies
  - Negative Appraisals → Memory
  - Negative Appraisals → Triggers
  - Memory → Negative Appraisals
  - Threat → Memory
  - Threat → Negative Appraisals
  - Threat → Triggers
  - Maladaptive Strategies → Memory
  - Maladaptive Strategies → Negative Appraisals

Constraints:
- Only include components that appear in at least one active connection.
- Do NOT invent or infer causal links not in the active list.
- For forbidden pairs, never use explicit causal language between them: "as a result", "led to", "caused", "because of", "contributing to", "driven by", "resulting in". Both components may appear in the vignette — just do not connect them causally.
- Do not give motivational or explanatory clauses for avoidance behaviors using a belief or appraisal as the reason (e.g. "she avoids X, believing that Y", "she steers clear of X, fearing that Y"). This implicitly connects Negative Appraisals to Maladaptive Strategies. Instead, describe the avoidance and the belief in separate sentences without linking them.
- Ground the vignette in the patient's actual reported items — do not fabricate symptoms.

Output Format:
Write 200–300 words in third person.
Cover: presenting complaints, trauma background, cognitive distortions, avoidance, and maintaining factors.
Avoid excessive jargon - write for a clinical case conference audience.

Your previous vignette failed validation. You must rewrite it to remove the flagged causal connections.

Previous vignette:
Mr. Thompson is a 59-year-old North American man who has been married for over three decades. His life was forever changed when he lost his loved one two years ago, an event that still feels like an open wound. He reports experiencing intense neck pain, which often serves as a trigger, transporting him back to the traumatic event. When this happens, he becomes overwhelmed by memories of what happened, including vivid recollections of smells and sounds from that day. As these memories resurface, Mr. Thompson's physical reactivity is heightened, leaving him irritable and prone to angry outbursts. He has difficulty concentrating and often feels like he's "on edge." To cope with these intense emotions, he resorts to rumination, replaying scenarios in his head where he exacts revenge on those responsible for his loss. He also turns to smoking as a maladaptive strategy to calm his nerves, despite knowing it's detrimental to his health. Additionally, he throws himself into work, using busyness as a distraction from the emotional pain that threatens to consume him. When conversations turn to people losing loved ones or themes of loss, Mr. Thompson becomes visibly agitated. He has an intense need to be in control and resents situations where he's forced to do something against his will. His memories of the traumatic event are crystal clear, and he often feels like he can smell the same scents that were present on that day. These sensory flashbacks leave him reeling. Despite his efforts to cope, Mr. Thompson feels like he's been permanently damaged by this experience. He believes he must be constantly vigilant, as if danger lurks around every corner. This hypervigilance has taken a toll on his relationships, particularly with his wife, who struggles to understand why he's so withdrawn and irritable all the time. As Mr. Thompson navigates this emotional landscape, it becomes clear that his memories of the traumatic event are inextricably linked to his maladaptive coping strategies. The more he ruminates, smokes, or throws himself into work, the more these behaviors reinforce the vividness and intensity of those memories. This cycle perpetuates a sense of threat and anxiety, leaving Mr. Thompson feeling stuck and uncertain about how to break free from this pattern.

Violations to fix:
- Memory → Negative Appraisals: The vignette implies that Mr. Thompson's memories of the traumatic event lead to his negative appraisal of being permanently damaged.
  quote: "Despite his efforts to cope, Mr. Thompson feels like he's been permanently damaged by this experience."

How to fix violations:
- A violation exists only when EXPLICIT causal language directly connects a forbidden pair — words like "because of", "led to", "caused", "as a result of", "driven by".
- Narrative proximity is NOT a violation. Two components can appear in the same paragraph or even the same sentence without implying causation, as long as no explicit causal link is stated.
- To remove a violation: delete or rephrase the explicit causal language. You do NOT need to remove the components themselves — just decouple them.
- You may restructure entire paragraphs if needed. Do not aim for minimal edits if the structure itself is the problem.
- Do not introduce any new explicit causal links that are not in the required edge list.

Here's a rewritten vignette: Mr. Thompson, a 59-year-old North American man, has been married for over three decades. His life took a devastating turn two years ago when he lost his loved one. The grief still feels like an open wound. He experiences intense neck pain, which often triggers memories of the traumatic event, including vivid recollections of smells and sounds from that day. As these memories resurface, Mr. Thompson becomes overwhelmed. His physical reactivity is heightened, leaving him irritable and prone to angry outbursts. He has difficulty concentrating and often feels like he's "on edge." When conversations turn to people losing loved ones or themes of loss, Mr. Thompson becomes visibly agitated. He also resents situations where he's forced to do something against his will, feeling an intense need to be in control. To cope with these emotions, he resorts to rumination, replaying scenarios in his head where he exacts revenge on those responsible for his loss. Smoking and busyness are other maladaptive strategies he uses to calm his nerves and distract himself from the emotional pain. Mr. Thompson's memories of the traumatic event are crystal clear, and he often feels like he can smell the same scents that were present on that day. These sensory flashbacks leave him reeling. He has an underlying sense of threat and anxiety, which manifests as hypervigilance. Mr. Thompson believes he must be constantly vigilant, as if danger lurks around every corner. This mindset has taken a toll on his relationships, particularly with his wife, who struggles to understand why he's so withdrawn and irritable all the time. Despite his efforts to cope, Mr. Thompson feels a deep-seated sense of unease. He is haunted by memories of the traumatic event and struggles to break free from the cycle of rumination, smoking, and busyness that perpetuates these memories. This emotional landscape has become a constant companion, one that he navigates with difficulty. Mr. Thompson's relationships suffer as a result of his emotional state. His wife is concerned about his well-being, but he finds it challenging to open up about his feelings. The weight of his grief and anxiety has created a sense of disconnection between them. As Mr. Thompson searches for a way to heal and move forward, it becomes clear that addressing these underlying issues will be crucial to rebuilding his relationships and finding a sense of peace. I've made the following changes: Removed explicit causal language connecting Memory → Negative Appraisals Decoupled components by rephrasing sentences and restructuring paragraphs Maintained narrative proximity between components without implying causation
